title: Masked Autoencoders Are Effective Tokenizers for Diffusion Models

reviews.summary: This paper studies what makes an effective latent space for latent diffusion models and argues that “structured” latent spaces with fewer effective mixture modes and more discriminative features ease diffusion training and improve image quality. It proposes MAE-Tok, a plain autoencoder trained with masked modeling and auxiliary shallow decoders to predict multi-level targets (pixels, HOG, DINOv2, CLIP, optional text BPE), followed by decoder-only fine-tuning to recover reconstruction fidelity. Empirically, MAE-Tok enables strong ImageNet 256×256 and 512×512 conditional generation with only 128 latent tokens, achieving competitive or SOTA gFID/IS and large speedups in training and inference; theoretically, the paper connects latent-mode complexity to diffusion sample complexity via existing results on GMMs.
strengths: Introduces a simple yet effective recipe to train continuous tokenizers: masked AE training with auxiliary decoders for multi-target reconstruction plus decoder-only fine-tuning, clearly decoupling “discriminative latent space” learning from “pixel fidelity.”

Provides a unifying perspective that connects latent mode structure to diffusion training difficulty; the use of GMM-fitting diagnostics and LP-vs-gFID correlation offers a practical lens to analyze tokenizers.
Uses 1D latent token design with learnable tokens for aggressive compression (128 tokens) at both 256 and 512 resolutions, simplifying the generative backbone’s workload.

Comprehensive ablations on masking, auxiliary target choice, mask ratio, and auxiliary decoder depth; includes linear probing, GMM-fitting NLL trends, UMAP visualizations, convergence comparisons, and unconditional generation metrics.
Strong system-level comparisons at both 256×256 and 512×512 ImageNet, with and without CFG, against a wide set of autoregressive and diffusion baselines.
Clear measurements of efficiency (e.g., FLOPs, throughput) and training-speed gains attributable to reduced token counts.
The motivation, method, and training schedule (mask modeling + decoder FT) are explained cleanly with a helpful architecture diagram and consistent notation.
Results are presented in a reproducible manner, with code release promised and many training details relegated to appendices.
Demonstrates that well-structured continuous latents can match or surpass RA-VAE/VAE-based pipelines for conditional ImageNet, using dramatically fewer tokens.
If broadly validated, the insight that latent structure (vs. variational constraints) is the key driver for diffusion performance may influence tokenizer design across modalities and tasks.
weaknesses: The theoretical link from “fewer GMM modes” to improved diffusion performance relies on composing prior theorems under restrictive assumptions (equally weighted, spherical GMMs with separation). The bridge from real-tokenizer latents to this setting is suggestive but not rigorous.

GMM “mode counting” is inferred indirectly via NLL vs. number of components; it can be sensitive to covariance assumptions, dimensionality, and sample size. There is no principled model selection (e.g., BIC/AIC, cross-validated likelihood) to estimate K.

The approach injects semantics via DINOv2/CLIP targets, which amounts to a form of representation alignment; the claim that “variational constraints are not necessary” may conflate “no KL” with “strong external semantic supervision,” and thus should be framed carefully.

Fairness of comparisons: many baselines (e.g., VAEs) are trained without access to the same external teacher features (DINOv2/CLIP). A fully matched baseline (VAE/RA-VAE with identical auxiliary targets and training budget) would strengthen claims.
The compute overhead of extracting DINO/CLIP features during tokenizer training is not reported in wall-clock terms; the net training-time benefits vs. teacher-free pipelines remain unclear.
The theory’s sample-complexity prediction could be stress-tested by varying dataset size for different tokenizers to see whether performance degrades as suggested (O(K^4))—this is currently missing.
Generalization beyond ImageNet class-conditional generation (e.g., class-agnostic datasets, text-to-image) is not demonstrated, despite optional text-BPE targets being proposed.

Some mathematical expressions suffer from minor formatting artifacts; more importantly, assumptions and their applicability to realistic latent distributions could be discussed more explicitly in the main text (not only appendices).
The exact training data used per tokenizer (ImageNet-only vs. LAION-COCO † variants) and their influence on subsequent diffusion results could be highlighted more prominently to avoid confusion.

While many tokenizers and alignment approaches are cited, a more explicit, apples-to-apples comparison to recent representation-aligned VAEs/continuous tokenizers using the same external teachers would contextualize the contribution.
Discussion of model selection for latent complexity (other than GMMs) and related diagnostics (e.g., DP-GMM, spectral clustering, information-theoretic metrics) is limited.
reviews.questions:
1. How much wall-clock overhead (time, GPU-hours, energy) does teacher feature extraction (DINOv2/CLIP, BPE) add to tokenizer training, and how does this affect the claimed end-to-end efficiency gains?
2. Can you provide a matched baseline where a VAE or RA-VAE is trained with exactly the same auxiliary targets and training schedule (including decoder FT) to isolate the effect of “no KL” vs. “semantic alignment”?
3. How sensitive are the GMM-fitting conclusions to covariance modeling (full vs. spherical), dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA), or selection criteria (BIC/AIC) for estimating the effective number of modes?
4. Can you validate the theoretical sample-complexity prediction by training diffusion models on downsampled ImageNet (e.g., 10%, 25%, 50%) with different tokenizers and tracking gFID vs. data size?
5. Do the conclusions extend to text-to-image or other datasets beyond ImageNet? If not, what failure modes have you observed when scaling to open-domain prompts?
6. What is the effect of removing CLIP/DINOv2 targets at 512×512 on final gFID/IS? Could a teacher-free variant still beat VAE baselines given the masking and decoder FT alone?
7. How does the number of learnable latent tokens (L) trade off with reconstruction and generation quality at 512×512? Any scaling trends to guide practitioners?
reviews.assessment: This is a timely and practically impactful paper that challenges the prevailing reliance on VAEs/quantization for latent diffusion tokenization, showing that a masked AE with auxiliary semantic targets can deliver highly structured latents, markedly faster convergence, and state-of-the-art ImageNet performance with just 128 tokens. The empirical story is compelling and well-substantiated by ablations and diagnostics, and the training recipe is simple and likely to be adopted. The main caveats are (i) the theoretical argument is more illustrative than definitive for real-world latents, and (ii) fairness concerns around external semantic supervision (DINO/CLIP) versus classical VAE baselines need more controlled comparisons and explicit reporting of compute overhead. Addressing these would further solidify the central claim that latent structure, not variational regularization, is the key driver for diffusion quality. Overall, I view this as a strong contribution suitable for ICML, with high potential value to the community.
